The laparoscopic cholecystectomy begins with the placement of an 11mm camera port and three additional ports: one in the epigastrium and two 5mm ports in the right hypochondrium along the mid-clavicular and mid-axillary lines. The mid-axillary port is used for fundal retraction, initiating the operation with posterior Calot's triangle dissection.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery or Maryland dissector to perform the dissection during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
Proper dissection with the hemostasis is usually done.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection of the gallbladder, using laparoscopic instruments through established ports in the epigastrium and right hypochondrium.
The main purpose is that to dissect the callot's triangle and it is usually done by

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct and cystic artery while achieving the critical view of safety. The posterior dissection is performed to ensure no structures extend from the gallbladder bed into the liver, with the critical view of safety clearly visible.
The surgeon visualizes the gallbladder and Calot's triangle, ensuring no structures extend into the liver. The cystic duct and cystic artery are carefully dissected using a Maryland dissector.

In the Calot's triangle, the surgeon achieves hemostasis after separating the cystic duct and cystic artery. Clips are applied with two proximally and one distally, followed by cutting the cystic duct and cystic artery.

The surgeon achieves proper hemostasis using bipolar, unipolar, and harmonic scalpels. After clipping and cutting the cystic duct and cystic artery, the gallbladder is dissected from the liver bed and removed.
The gallbladder is removed through the epigastric or umbilical port and prepared for histopathological examination.
